straight_beat.name: Hal Cifuentes
straight_beat.column:
The Angels won yesterday, which is good. They beat the Reds, 9-6, which is better. But here’s the box score talking: the bullpen coughed up two runs in the eighth, the offense went cold in the late innings, and the team’s record sits at 8-8, 0.5 games behind the AL West. You know the script.  

The win was a relief, sure. A walk-off home run by someone, maybe? No, not quite. The Angels’ offense did its thing in the first five innings—four runs, a few hits, a few strikeouts—but then it sputtered. The Reds came back with a couple of runs in the sixth, and the Angels’ bats couldn’t find the barrel after that. You’d think a team that’s scored 100 runs in August would have more consistency, but here we are. The same old story: hot starts, cold finishes.  

The bullpen’s the real problem. They’ve been a mess all year, and yesterday was no exception. The Reds’ eighth-inning rally? It started with a double, a walk, and a wild pitch. Then a run-scoring single. Then a bases-loaded walk. The relievers didn’t have answers. You can’t rely on a bullpen that’s allowed more than four runs in four of their last five games. The manager’s decision to go with the long

optimist.name: Sunny Escalante
optimist.column:
The Angels lost yesterday, but let’s not bury the lead. They didn’t just lose a game—they lost a game while still managing to make you believe in the Rally Monkey’s divine plan. Sure, it was a 9-1 loss, but the way Rengifo flipped that double play in the third? That’s October magic hiding in April. You could see it in the way the team kept swinging, even when the Reds were pulling ahead. Baseball is a cruel game, but it’s also a stubborn one. The Angels kept fighting, and that’s what matters.  

Now, let’s talk about the pitching. It wasn’t perfect—no team is ever perfect in a 9-6 loss—but there was grit in the wind. The starters didn’t fold, and the bullpen held the line until the late innings. You can’t always control the hits, but you can control how you respond. And respond they did. The offense, for all its inconsistencies, showed flashes of the firepower that made this team a contender. Look at the way they kept chipping away in the late innings, like they were trying to tell the Reds,

pessimist.name: Murray Doblinski
pessimist.column:
The Angels won again. Nine runs. Nine. And somewhere Arte is laughing into a glass of Cabernet, counting the money Shohei isn’t making him.  

It’s the same old Halos.  

You think the 8-8 record is a sign of progress? Please. The AL West is a sieve, and the Angels are just a grain of sand in the bottom of it. They’re 0.5 games behind, sure, but that’s only because the rest of the division is collectively doing worse than a guy who thinks he’s a baseball analyst. The Astros are still a disaster, the Rangers are still a disaster, and the Mariners? They’re a disaster that’s pretending to be a contender. But the Angels? They’re just… here. Again.  

Look, the offense showed up yesterday. Shohei Ohtani did his thing, the guys in the middle of the order finally figured out how to hit a fastball, and the bullpen—oh, the bullpen—gave up three runs in the ninth. Wait, no, they gave up three runs in the ninth? That’s not a bullpen problem. That’s a problem with a bullpen that’s been asked to do too much and hasn’t been given the tools to do it properly.  

Arte Moreno
